content:
9 Best Dog Toys for Puppies – Teething & Training Picks Tested (2026)

Puppies chew everything — your shoes, furniture legs, phone chargers, and their own beds. The right chew toy redirects this natural behavior while soothing teething pain and building good habits. We tested 9 puppy toys across three categories (teething, interactive, and training) with puppies aged 8 weeks to 6 months to find the safest and most engaging options.

Why Puppy-Specific Toys Matter

Adult dog toys are often too hard for developing puppy teeth and jaws. Puppies lose their baby teeth between 3–6 months, during which gums become swollen and painful. Toys designed for puppies are softer, smaller, and shaped for puppy mouths. Using adult-strength chew toys can crack baby teeth and create lifelong dental problems.

Beyond teething, the right toys during puppyhood shape adult behavior. Puppies who learn to chew appropriate objects rarely develop destructive chewing habits as adults. Interactive toys build confidence, reduce anxiety, and provide mental stimulation during the critical development window.

Types of Puppy Toys

Teething Toys

Soft rubber, textured surfaces, and freezeable designs that soothe inflamed gums. Best for puppies 3–6 months during active teething. Look for multi-textured surfaces that massage gums from different angles.

Interactive Toys

Toys that respond to puppy actions — squeaking, bouncing, or dispensing treats. Build problem-solving skills and prevent boredom. Essential for puppies left alone during work hours.

Training Toys

Tug ropes, fetch balls, and treat-stuffable toys that support obedience training. Use as positive reinforcement rewards during training sessions.

Best Puppy Toys by Development Stage

8–12 Weeks (New Home Adjustment)

Soft plush toys with heartbeat simulators help puppies transition from littermates. Crinkle toys satisfy curiosity without overwhelming sensitive ears. Avoid hard toys — baby teeth are fragile and gums aren't ready for firm chewing yet.

12–16 Weeks (Early Teething)

Introduce textured rubber toys and freezeable teethers as gums start swelling. Cold items numb pain naturally without medication. Rotate 3 teething toys daily to maintain novelty and engagement.

16–24 Weeks (Peak Teething)

This is when chewing intensity peaks. Provide 4–5 approved chew options and redirect furniture chewing immediately. Puppy Kongs stuffed with frozen peanut butter provide 20–30 minutes of focused chewing per session.

Safety Guidelines

  • Size matters: Toys should be large enough that puppies can't swallow them whole
  • Material check: BPA-free rubber, organic cotton, or food-grade silicone only
  • Supervised play: Especially with rope toys and plush toys until you know your puppy's chewing style
  • Replace when damaged: Small pieces from torn toys are choking hazards
  • Avoid rawhide: Splinters and intestinal blockages are serious risks for puppies

Frequently Asked Questions

How many toys does a puppy need?

5–8 toys rotated in groups of 3. This keeps toys feeling new without overwhelming your puppy. Include at least one teething toy, one interactive toy, and one comfort toy in each rotation.

When do puppies stop teething?

Most puppies finish teething by 6–7 months when all 42 adult teeth are in. Chewing intensity decreases significantly after this period, though some dogs remain active chewers into adulthood.

Are rope toys safe for puppies?

Only with supervision. Puppies that shred and swallow rope fibers risk intestinal blockages. Use rope toys for supervised tug-of-war games only, and replace them when fraying starts.
